1: 'I looked, and I saw in the expanse above the heads of the cherubim something like a throne of lapis lazuli appearing above them' — the chariot-throne vision in the temple context. The lapis lazuli (sapphire) throne of 1:26 reappears, now situated above the cherubim in the temple rather than above the Chebar canal. The same throne of judgment and glory that appeared at Ezekiel's commission now appears at Jerusalem for the departure of the glory. The divine throne's mobility — appearing at the river and at the temple — anticipates the NT's insistence that the divine presence is not confined to sacred geography (John 4:21-24).
2: 'He said to the man clothed in linen: Go in among the wheels beneath the cherubim; fill your hands with burning coals from among the cherubim and scatter them over the city' — the divine scribe who marked the faithful (Ezekiel 9) now becomes the agent of judgment-fire over the city. The coals from the divine chariot-throne scattered over Jerusalem recall the altar coal placed on Isaiah's lips for cleansing (Isaiah 6:6-7). Fire from the divine presence is both purifying and consuming: the same fire that cleanses the lips of the prophet consumes the city that refused cleansing.
3: 'The cherubim were standing on the south side of the temple when the man went in, and a cloud filled the inner court' — the cloud of divine glory filling the inner court as the glory prepares to depart. The cloud of YHWH's presence (the Shekinah) filling the space is simultaneously the most intense manifestation of divine presence and the precursor to the most significant departure. The cloud that filled Solomon's temple at dedication (1 Kings 8:10-11) now fills the temple one last time before leaving.
4: 'Then the glory of the LORD rose from above the cherubim and moved to the threshold of the temple. The cloud filled the temple, and the court was full of the radiance of the LORD's glory' — the first stage of the glory's departure: from above the cherubim to the threshold. The radiance (nogah, H5051) filling the court — the surrounding brightness of the divine presence — is the same radiance that will mark the New Jerusalem: 'the city does not need the sun or the moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light' (Revelation 21:23).
5: 'The sound of the wings of the cherubim could be heard as far away as the outer court, like the voice of God Almighty when he speaks' — the thunderous sound of the departing glory. The comparison to 'the voice of God Almighty' (qol El Shaddai) invokes the divine name of patriarchal revelation (Exodus 6:3; Genesis 17:1). The sound accompanying the glory's departure is the same voice that spoke to the patriarchs — YHWH's presence withdrawing while his voice is still heard.
6: 'When the LORD commanded the man clothed in linen: Take fire from among the wheels, from among the cherubim, the man went in and stood beside a wheel' — the man in linen standing beside the wheel to take the judgment-coals. The wheels of the divine chariot carry the fire of divine judgment — the same wheels that appeared in Ezekiel 1 as the moving spirit of the chariot. The fire taken from the wheels is fire with the full authority of the divine throne behind it.
7: 'Then one of the cherubim reached out his hand to the fire that was among them. He took some of it and put it into the hands of the man in linen, who took it and went out' — the cherub placing the fire in the human hands of the linen-clad figure. The divine fire mediated through angelic hands to human hands — the chain of divine authority. Christ as the mediator of divine fire: 'I have come to bring fire on the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled!' (Luke 12:49). The fire of the Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2:3: tongues as of fire) comes through the mediation of the risen and ascended Christ.
8: 'Under the wings of the cherubim could be seen what appeared to be human hands' — the hands of the cherubim: the divine chariot has human-form elements alongside the animal-form elements. The divine chariot bears the marks of humanity (hands) as well as the signs of transcendence (wings, wheels, fire). Christ, fully human and fully divine, embodies permanently what the chariot-vision displays symbolically.
9: 'I looked and saw four wheels beside the cherubim, one beside each cherub; the wheels sparkled like topaz' — the topaz wheels of the divine chariot. Topaz (tarshish, H8658, a precious stone) sparkle: the divine mobility is precious, beautiful, and aligned with the throne's glory. The wheels of the chariot moving in four directions simultaneously represent the omnipresence of the divine sovereignty — no direction is closed to YHWH's movement.
10: 'As for their appearance, the four of them looked alike; each was like a wheel intersecting a wheel' — the wheel-within-wheel construction representing movement in any direction without turning. The divine chariot can move instantly in any direction — the representation of divine omnipresence and sovereign freedom. Christ's resurrection body moves with the same freedom: appearing through locked doors (John 20:19), ascending and sitting at the right hand (Acts 1:9-10; Hebrews 1:3), coming again in glory (Matthew 24:30).
11: 'As they moved, they would go in any one of the four directions the cherubim faced; the wheels did not turn about as the cherubim went, for the cherubim could go in whichever direction they chose without the wheels turning' — the coordinated movement of cherubim and wheels: perfect unity of will and motion. The divine chariot-throne is a picture of perfect unity between the divine will and its execution. Christ as the one in whom divine will is perfectly executed: 'I have come down from heaven not to do my will but to do the will of him who sent me' (John 6:38).
12: 'Their entire bodies, including their backs, their hands, and their wings, were completely full of eyes, as were their four wheels' — the all-seeing eyes covering the bodies and wheels of the divine chariot: nothing escapes the divine gaze. Revelation 4:6 describes the four living creatures around the throne as 'covered with eyes, in front and in back' — the same total-vision characteristic. Christ as the one 'to whom all are naked and exposed' (Hebrews 4:13) embodies the all-seeing divine gaze that the chariot-throne displays.
13: 'I heard the wheels being called the whirling wheels — these were the terms I heard' — the naming of the wheels: galgal (H1534, wheel/whirling) for the wheels of the chariot that move with supernatural speed. The whirling quality of the divine chariot's wheels represents the speed of divine action — YHWH moves with absolute swiftness when the moment for action arrives. Contrast the 'slowness' that false prophets attributed to divine action (Ezekiel 12:22-28): the whirling wheels refute the 'the days drag on' proverb.
14: 'Each of the cherubim had four faces: the first face was a cherub's face, the second was a human face, the third the face of a lion, and the fourth the face of an eagle' — the four-faced cherubim. In Ezekiel 1 the four faces were: lion, ox, human, eagle (Ezekiel 1:10). Here the ox-face is replaced by a cherub-face. The four faces correspond closely to the four living creatures of Revelation 4:7: lion, ox/calf, human, eagle — the faces that traditional Christian interpretation connects to the four gospel writers (Matthew/human, Mark/lion, Luke/ox, John/eagle). The divine chariot's faces become the faces of the four gospel testimonies to Christ.
15: 'The cherubim rose upward — these were the living creatures I had seen by the Chebar River' — the explicit identification of the temple-vision cherubim with the Chebar-canal living creatures. The same divine chariot-throne that appeared in Babylonian exile returns to Jerusalem for the glory's departure. The theological implication: YHWH's chariot-throne moves freely between Jerusalem and Babylon, between the sacred center and the place of exile. Christ, present with his disciples in Galilee, Jerusalem, Emmaus, and the seashore after the resurrection, embodies this same freedom.
16: 'When the cherubim moved, the wheels beside them moved; and when the cherubim spread their wings to rise from the ground, the wheels did not leave their side' — the perfect coordination of the cherubim and wheels: the created beings and the divine chariot's mechanical components move in perfect unity. The Spirit's coordination of the body of Christ (1 Corinthians 12:12-13) reflects this same principle: diverse members moving in unified response to the same divine will.
17: 'When the cherubim stood still, they also stood still; and when the cherubim rose, they rose with them, for the spirit of the living creatures was in them' — 'the spirit of the living creatures was in them' (ruach hachayyah bahem): the animating Spirit within the wheels. The divine chariot moves because the Spirit animates it from within. Paul applies the same interior-Spirit logic to believers: 'If the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead is living in you, he who raised Christ from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies because of his Spirit who lives in you' (Romans 8:11).
18: 'Then the glory of the LORD departed from over the threshold of the temple and stopped above the cherubim' — the glory leaving the threshold (where it paused in Ezekiel 10:4) and returning above the cherubim. The departure is progressive, intentional, and painful. Christ's departure from Jerusalem follows the same pattern: the gradual withdrawal (growing opposition, rejected warnings) culminating in the lamentation 'your house is left to you desolate' (Matthew 23:38). The glory that departs will return.
19: 'While I watched, the cherubim spread their wings and rose from the ground, and as they went, the wheels went with them. They stopped at the entrance of the east gate of the LORD's house, and the glory of the God of Israel was above them' — the cherubim at the east gate with the glory above them: the final position within the temple precinct before the full departure (Ezekiel 11:22-23) to the Mount of Olives. The east gate that is 'shut' in Ezekiel 44:1-2 after the glory returns is the same gate from which the glory departed. Christ's ascension from the Mount of Olives (Acts 1:9-12) and his predicted return 'in the same way' (Acts 1:11) from the east fulfills this departure pattern.
20: 'These were the living creatures I had seen beneath the God of Israel by the River Chebar, and I knew they were cherubim' — the explicit identification complete: the river-creatures = the temple-creatures = cherubim. The consistency of the divine chariot across two visions (Chebar and Jerusalem) and across the entire arc of the book (exile → judgment → restoration) demonstrates that the same God who appears in Babylon appears in Jerusalem and will appear in the new temple. 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8).
21: 'Each had four faces and four wings, and under their wings was what looked like human hands' — the summary description of the cherubim: four faces (divine, human, animal, celestial), four wings (covering, moving, honoring), and human hands (acting, receiving, serving). The divine chariot bears the permanent marks of both transcendence and humanness — anticipating the Incarnation in which the transcendent takes on the human permanently.
22: 'Their faces had the same appearance as those I had seen by the Chebar River — each went straight ahead' — the final note of consistency and directness: 'each went straight ahead' (el ever penehem yelekhu). The straight-ahead movement of the divine chariot, without deviation or turning back, points to the intentionality of YHWH's purposes. The mission of the Son was similarly without deviation: 'he steadfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem' (Luke 9:51). The chariot that departs from the temple goes straight — and it will return straight, from the same direction (Ezekiel 43:1-2).
